national_expectations.summary: After surviving a dramatic penalty shootout against the Netherlands, Moroccan and pan-Arab press have doubled down on the idea that this squad can go beyond 2022, with coach Mohamed Ouahbi repeating that the goal is to go further than the Qatar semi-final and saying the win means 'everyone respects Morocco now.' Meeting co-host Canada, ranked far below Morocco in FIFA's table, in the last 16 is being framed domestically as a highly winnable step toward the quarter-finals, while coverage still stresses not underestimating the occasion or Canada's home crowd.

national_mood.summary: Jubilant and drained after an extraordinary round-of-32 shootout: Issa Diop's stoppage-time header forced extra time, five penalties were missed across both sides (including one from captain Achraf Hakimi, who struck the post), Dutch keeper Bart Verbruggen deflected a Moroccan kick into his own net, and Yassine Bounou's save of Crysencio Summerville's attempt sent Ismael Saibari through to score the winner. Pan-Arab press quoted via Hespress (Tunisian outlets Al-Shorouk, Al-Maghreb, Al-Sabah, Lokuotidian) framed the result as proof Morocco is a genuine 'global football power,' with one outlet calling Bounou's shootout display 'the birth of a new school in goalkeeping.' Moroccan coverage credits mental strength and composure across two knockout rounds now won.

manager_situation.summary: Mohamed Ouahbi — the Belgium-born former Under-20 World Cup-winning coach who replaced Walid Regragui in March 2026 — has now delivered his first senior knockout win, further cementing his standing. He credited the result to patience and belief, said his side handled an unexpectedly defensive Dutch set-up ('We were surprised to see the Netherlands play with so many defenders'), praised his substitutes and younger players, and reiterated in mid-June that his ambition is to surpass the 2022 semi-final rather than merely repeat it.

historical_context.summary: Morocco carry the enduring legacy of their 2022 semi-final run, the first by an African nation, and coverage now credits them with reaching the knockouts and winning a knockout match in consecutive World Cups. Their next opponent, Canada, was beaten 2-1 by Morocco in the Qatar 2022 group stage en route to that run. Morocco goalkeeper Yassine Bounou (Bono) was born in Montreal and, as a young player, turned down an approach from a former Canada coach to represent Canada instead — 'representing my national team was something I had very deep inside,' he has said — adding a personal thread to the rematch on North American soil in Houston.

dominant_storylines.storyline: 'Morocco does it again': a stoppage-time equaliser and a wild shootout keep the Qatar 2022 magic alive. Issa Diop's 91st-minute header forced extra time, then Morocco won 3-2 on penalties in a shootout that saw five misses in total — including a post-hitting attempt from captain Hakimi — plus a bizarre own goal from Dutch keeper Bart Verbruggen, before Bounou's save of Summerville's kick let Saibari clinch it.

Ouahbi frames the win as validation and doubles down on ambition, saying the team 'completely controlled the match' with a commanding hold on possession over the Netherlands, and that Morocco has now 'gained everybody's respect' — while continuing to repeat, first stated in mid-June, that he wants to go further than the 2022 semi-final rather than just match it.

A personal rematch angle vs. Canada: goalkeeper Yassine Bounou was born in Montreal and once turned down interest from a former Canada coach to represent his adopted country instead; Morocco also beat Canada 2-1 in the Qatar 2022 group stage on the way to the semi-finals, giving this last-16 meeting a nostalgic, confidence-building framing in Moroccan press.

Pan-Arab press reception: a Hespress round-up of Tunisian newspapers (Al-Shorouk, Al-Maghreb, Al-Sabah, Lokuotidian) hailed Morocco as a 'global football power not to be underestimated' after eliminating 'one of Europe's football giants,' with commentary suggesting the run could feed into 'Africa's first World Cup' narrative, and singling out Bounou's penalty-saving performance as exceptional.

Ismael Saibari's breakout tournament continues: after scoring in the group stage (including the tournament's fastest goal, versus Scotland) and drawing reported Bayern Munich transfer interest, he stepped up to score the winning penalty against the Netherlands, reinforcing his status as Morocco's standout performer of the tournament so far.

attributed_quotes.quote: It is a fully deserved victory in every sense of the word.
attributed_quotes.context: Post-match reaction after Morocco's 3-2 penalty-shootout win over the Netherlands in the round of 32.

attributed_quotes.quote: We were surprised to see the Netherlands play with so many defenders.
attributed_quotes.context: On having to adapt tactically to the Netherlands' five-man defensive setup.

attributed_quotes.quote: This did not happen because of words. It happened because of the work we have done over many years.
attributed_quotes.context: On the claim that 'everyone respects Morocco now' after beating the Netherlands.

attributed_quotes.quote: If you offered me another semi-final like we managed in 2022, I'd tell you that I want to go further.
attributed_quotes.context: On not being satisfied with repeating the 2022 semi-final run, ahead of the knockout rounds.

attributed_quotes.quote: Representing my national team was something I had very deep inside.
attributed_quotes.context: On having been born in Montreal and once approached about representing Canada before choosing Morocco, ahead of facing Canada in the round of 16.

gaps: A Hespress training report on a minor reported knock for defender Shadi Riad (said not to be a concern) is dated 2026-07-02, after this file's 2026-07-01 research cutoff, so it is deliberately excluded here; should be checked and added if still current at the next refresh.

No verbatim reaction quotes from Morocco outfield players (Saibari, Diop, Hakimi, Mazraoui) about the Netherlands shootout itself could be sourced within the cutoff — only coach Ouahbi's press-conference quotes and one older Bounou quote (from pre-tournament reporting on his Canada connection) were available.

No verbatim original-language (Arabic/French) quote text could be confirmed exactly as spoken; all quotes used here come from English-language outlets or English renderings, so quote_original is null throughout. If exact Arabic-language originals are later confirmed (e.g., from Hespress interviews), they should be added with lang tags.

The Tunisian press reactions (Al-Shorouk, Al-Maghreb, Al-Sabah, Lokuotidian) are relayed via a Hespress round-up rather than the primary Tunisian outlets directly, so they are summarized as attributed context/storyline rather than presented as standalone sourced quotes.

Spelling of some Moroccan players varies across outlets (e.g. El Aynaoui; Rahimi vs. Rhaimi for the penalty-taker whose kick was turned into an own goal by Verbruggen) — the most common English-language spellings were used.

No FIFA/CAF official (fact-grade) statement specific to the Canada round-of-16 tie was located; all expectation/mood content for this round remains press-sourced (discovery tier), consistent with the R32 file.
Direct Moroccan federation (FRMF) comment on the Canada tie was not located; manager and press framing used instead.

source_data_snapshot.note: REVIEWER NOTE (outlet not on the pre-built allowlist, no Canada region listed): TSN is Canada's dedicated national sports broadcaster (rights-holder for CFL/NHL/soccer coverage), a mainstream credible outlet comparable in tier to BBC Sport/Sky Sports; used for Bounou Montreal-birth/Floro anecdote and the 2022 Qatar 2-1 head-to-head, not for odds or predictions.
